era_context: The Late Roman Republic in its final decades of instability. The Social War (91–88 BCE) has recently concluded; Sulla's dictatorship (82–79 BCE) has ended with his death in 78 BCE, leaving the Republic fractured between optimates and populares factions. In the East, the Seleucid Empire is fragmenting; Pontus under Mithridates VI remains a major power. Egypt is ruled by the Ptolemaic dynasty under Ptolemy XII. In China, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Xuan is consolidating power after the turmoil of the Changxin Marquis affair.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (no written records for specific years; Maya Classic period ongoing but undated to individual years)

events.title: Sertorian War continues in Hispania
events.description: The civil war between the Lusitanian general Sertorius and the Sullan faction under Pompey the Great continues in its fifth year. Sertorius, backed by Lusitanian and Celtiberian forces, maintains control of much of Hispania against Roman legions. This conflict represents a major challenge to the optimates' consolidation of power after Sulla's death and will not be fully resolved until Sertorius's assassination in 72 BCE.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (fragmentary for this period)

events.certainty_note: The Sertorian War is well-attested in multiple sources. The war lasted 80–72 BCE; specific campaigns in 74 BCE are documented in later historical accounts, though no contemporary records survive.

events.title: Third Mithridatic War: Lucullus campaigns in Pontus
events.description: Lucius Licinius Lucullus, appointed commander against Mithridates VI, begins his campaign in Pontus. The Third Mithridatic War (75–63 BCE) is now in its second year. Lucullus will prove to be an exceptionally capable general, eventually driving Mithridates from his kingdom, though the war will be protracted and politically contentious in Rome.
events.certainty_note: The Third Mithridatic War is well-documented. Lucullus's appointment and early campaigns are attested in multiple sources, though specific battles in 74 BCE are sometimes conflated with adjacent years.

events.title: Gladiatorial uprising of Spartacus begins (traditional dating)
events.description: According to some ancient sources, the famous slave revolt led by Spartacus begins in 74 BCE at the gladiatorial school of Batiatus in Capua. However, the dating of this event is disputed; other sources place the outbreak in 73 BCE. The revolt will become one of the most significant slave uprisings in Roman history, lasting until 71 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The start date of Spartacus's revolt is disputed between 74 and 73 BCE in ancient sources. Most modern scholarship favors 73 BCE, but some ancient authorities (including Livy) place it in 74 BCE. The uncertainty reflects the loss of contemporary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Spartacus's revolt is the primary point of contention. While some ancient sources (notably Livy) place the outbreak in 74 BCE, the majority of later sources and modern scholarship favor 73 BCE. The discrepancy may reflect confusion in ancient chronologies or the loss of precise contemporary records. Additionally, the historical significance of the Sertorian War in 74 BCE is sometimes overshadowed in modern accounts by the later prominence of Pompey, potentially skewing the relative importance assigned to events in this year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 74 BCE is moderate but heavily dependent on later compilations. Plutarch and Appian, writing in the 1st–2nd centuries CE, are the primary sources for events of this year, but they drew on earlier (now lost) sources and sometimes conflate or misdate events. No contemporary Roman records survive for this year. The sources are generally favorable to the optimates faction and may underrepresent the perspectives of Sertorius or the slave rebels. Archaeological evidence from Capua and Pontus provides some corroboration but does not resolve dating ambiguities.

graph_edges.note: The Sertorian War continues until Sertorius's assassination in 72 BCE
Lucullus's campaign is part of the Third Mithridatic War (75–63 BCE)
Spartacus's revolt (if begun in 74 BCE) continues until 71 BCE
